text: and it's been a number of weeks since we've had our last study in the Wednesday night series, The Names of God. And if you were keeping track of that particular series, this would be lesson number 15 with regard to that series of The Names of God. And as a reminder, we launched into this series in order to develop a deeper understanding of the nature and character of our God. And I'm so thankful that God wants us to know Him. Isn't that wonderful? And the reality is that we read of Moses and of Paul making the statements that they wanted to know God in a deeper, more intimate way. And that ought to be the desire of every child of God, that we desire to know Him in a deeper, more intimate way. But if you think about it from the opposite perspective, a great blessing is the fact that God wants us to know Him. He has given to us a Bible. He has given to us truth, and He's made it available to us. And what we know to be true about God is dependent upon our relationship with the Word of God. And you know, there's a number of people that don't know much about God. They don't know who God is. They have a wrong perspective of who God is because they don't go to a Bible-preaching church. They don't read a good Bible that reveals to them truth. They're gathering their facts, their ideas from other places, other sources. And so we find that it's our desire to have this deeper understanding of who our God is. The psalmist declares, you remember this verse, Psalm 9 and verse number 10? And the psalmist says this, And they that know thy name will put their trust in thee. And so you just pause and think about that for a moment. They that know thy name will put their trust in thee, for thou, Lord, hast not forsaken them that seek thee. And the reality is that as we know God and we know Him in a deeper, more intimate way, and we understand His character and His nature, the more we will trust Him, the greater faith we will have. Why? Because we know the God of the impossible. We know the God who can calm the storms. We know the God who can move the mountains, amen? And what a blessing to understand who He is. This evening, our study will take us here to Genesis chapter 16. And we'll know a name that is actually representative here of a interaction where the scriptures are telling us about Hagar. And Hagar uses this name, and it's here in Genesis chapter 16. And look in verse number 13, and we'll catch the whole context here in just a few moments. But notice in verse 13, the Bible says, and she, that's speaking of Hagar, and she called the name of the Lord that spake unto her, thou, God, seest me. For she said, have I also here looked after him that seeth me? El-Roy, that is the name that's here, thou, God, seest me. And so El-Roy, the God who sees me. I want you to think about that just for a moment this evening as we study through this passage and look at a number of other portions of scripture. And as we look here in Genesis chapter number 16, if you're taking notes, consider first of all what Hagar recognized. In the midst of some very difficult circumstances, the Lord appeared to her and spoke to her. Begin reading with me in verse number one of this chapter. Many of us are familiar with the passage. The Bible says, now Sarai, Abram's wife, bear him no children. And she had a handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar. We don't have time to develop all of this tonight, but we understand that God had given a promise to Abraham with regard to children, with regard to a son, a child. We understand also that when Abram followed the leadership of God, that he arrived in the land and there was a time of famine and he went to Egypt. And no doubt in my mind, as you study the scriptures, where Hagar enters into the picture is while they were down in Egypt and they came out of Egypt and Hagar is then with them. Notice in verse two, and Sarai said unto Abram, behold now, the Lord hath restrained me from bearing. I pray thee, go in unto my maid. It may be that I may obtain children by her. And Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai. And Sarai, Abram's wife, took Hagar, her maid, the Egyptian, after Abram had dwelt 10 years in the land of Canaan and gave to her her husband Abram to be his wife. And he went in unto Hagar and she conceived. And when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes. In verse five, and Sarai said unto Abram, my wrong be upon thee. I have given my maid into thy bosom. And when she saw that she had conceived, I was despised in her eyes. The Lord judged between me and thee. But Abram said unto Sarai, behold, thy maid is in thy hand. Do to her as it pleaseth thee. And when Sarai dealt hardly with her, she fled from her face. And I'm not, again, this evening, I preached through Genesis 16 a while ago. I'm not gonna deal with all of the contextual implications of all that's going on, but I want you to notice the difficult circumstances in which Hagar finds herself. And first of all, we understand that it was Sarai that came up with the plan in verse number two. It's not that Hagar was doing anything wicked or ungodly and casting herself upon Abram. We find, as we've read here in the context with regard to what's happened, we also acknowledge that Abram and Sarai had been in the land for 10 years and they'd had a promise from God that had not yet been fulfilled. And understand with me this evening that God's timing is not always our timing and we need to be careful not to get ahead of God and not to depend on the flesh and that's what's happened here. And we find now that Sarai has this issue with Hagar because she's now conceived and Sarai is barren. And so we find in verse six at the end, as I just read, that she, Sarai, dealt hardly with her Hagar. But notice verse seven. The Bible says, and the angel of the Lord found her, that's Hagar, by a fountain of water in the wilderness, by the fountain in the way to Shur. And he said, Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence comest thou, camest thou, excuse me, and whither wilt thou go? And she said, I flee from the face of my mistress Sarai. And the angel of the Lord said unto her, return to thy mistress and submit thyself under her hands. And the angel of the Lord said unto her, I will multiply thy seed exceedingly, but it shall not be numbered for multitude. And the angel of the Lord said unto her, behold, thou art with child, and shalt bear a son, and shalt call his name Ishmael, because the Lord hath heard thy affliction. And he will be a wild man, and will be against every man, and every man's hand against him, and he shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren. And she, the verse that we read by way of introduction this evening, verse 13, and she called the name of the Lord, that spake unto her, thou, God, seest me. For she said, have I also here looked after him that seeth me? Wherefore, the well was called Beer-le-Hi-Roi. Behold, it is between Kadesh and Bered. And Hagar bare Abram a son, and Abram called his son's name, which Hagar bare Ishmael. And Abram was fourscorn six years old when Hagar bare Ishmael to Abram. We consider what Hagar recognized in the midst of these very difficult circumstances that we've just read through the text of scripture, that God appeared to her, and God spoke to her, and she realized at that time, as the Lord speaks to her, she realized that though she felt helpless and though she felt alone, that God knew exactly where she was. That not only did God know where she was, but God knew what she was facing. God knew her circumstances. God knew all that had transpired. God saw where she was. And we begin to consider what she recognized and how she responds in verse 13 by saying that she called the name of the Lord that spake unto her, thou God, seest me. And that's where we see the name tonight, Elroi, the God who sees me. You know, we look at the context here in Genesis 16 and the use of this name for God, but I want you, as we consider what Hagar recognized and seek to make some application in our own lives with regard to the name of God, Elroi, I want you to consider these truths and rest in the God who sees you. The God who sees you. You ever heard somebody say, no one knows, no one cares, no one sees, no one understands? And sometimes you hear people say those words. Sometimes the circumstances of your life and my life and the circumstances of those that we know, sometimes that we're familiar with, that we're maybe just simply acquainted with, that they're dealing with some serious life circumstances. And sometimes in the midst of those circumstances, they can feel as if they're all alone. They can feel as if no one understands, no one sees. But I'm thankful this evening that. though we can find ourselves in difficult times and difficult circumstances, that we can rest in the knowledge this evening that God gives us in his word that he is the God who sees. He's the God who sees. And that ought to bring for each of us this evening a measure of comfort that ought to cause us to rest in knowing that God sees, that God knows, that he's aware of. And to think about this truth this evening as we look at this name for God, and I understand and we won't read any of the verses. There's so many of them that we could turn to that it's hard to even just select a sampling of them. But throughout the era of Israel's history that we would refer to as the King era, in which you have all of the kings of Israel and Judah, and obviously you can study those through the book of 1 and 2 Samuel, 1 and 2 Kings, 1 and 2 Chronicles, and you can read passage after passage of this era of Israel's history. You'll note something that is repeated throughout those portions of scripture, and here's what's repeated throughout there, and there are a number of references where the Bible speaks of the king by name and says that their behavior, whether good or bad, in the eyes of the Lord. In the eyes of the Lord. And you'll see that oft repeated as it references the king and their behavior, and they did that which was right in the eyes of the Lord, in the sight of the Lord. They did that which was evil in the eyes of the Lord, in the sight of the Lord. And again, even as we would just reference so many of those portions of scripture this evening, and again not going to any of them in particular, but simply stating the fact that there are so many there that we could look to, the reality is that it makes us aware of the fact that God sees, that God knows, that He's aware. And you know, we live in a time in which it's kind of interesting. I was thinking about this the last couple of days. There are times in which people will say something like I just said a moment ago, no one sees, no one understands, but then there are times that people do something and they say, I hope no one saw that. You ever think about that? It's how it's, I hope no one sees that, I hope no one was watching, I hope no one, did not Moses hope that no one was watching when he committed an act of wickedness? Yeah, he looked this way and he looked that way and he thought no one was looking, but then God used him to write about it. Think about that for a moment. God sees everything. He knows everything. He is the God who sees me. And I want to look at a few verses this evening, look at Psalms, if you will, we'll look at Psalm 11. We can look at so many verses tonight, I've tried to pare it down so that we could just look at a sampling of these to be encouraged tonight to rest in the fact that we serve the Lord God Almighty and one of the names that's ascribed to Him is Elroy, the God who sees me. Psalm 11, the psalmist says in verse number one, in the Lord put I my trust. Now remember I just mentioned it by way of introduction, Psalm 9 in verse 10 says, and they that know thy name will put their trust in thee. Notice this, His eyes behold, His eyelids try the children of men. God sees. In Psalm 34, turn over just a few pages to Psalm 34 and notice in verse number 15, this specific psalm, a psalm ascribed to David, a psalm really of great rejoicing, how God delivered Him. He starts out speaking how He's going to praise and rejoice and He says, I'll bless the Lord at all times, His praise shall continually be in my mouth, hallelujah, that's where we ought to live. Why?
